title: Opposition Movements Responding to the Iran War
content:
On day one of the recent U.S.-Israeli military campaign, President Donald Trump urged the Iranian people to "take over your government." As the conflict rages on, identifying who actually constitutes the organized opposition within Iran remains a highly complex and fragmented issue. Historically, the most organized opposition groups have been the same factions that competed for national power during the 1979 revolution. Reza Pahlavi, the eldest son of the overthrown Shah, has lived in exile in the U.S. for his entire life but retains a strong presence via satellite television. Though his name is sometimes chanted at local protests, restoring the monarchy remains a highly contentious idea among diverse Iranian democratic activists.

Another significant faction is the Mojahedin-e-Khalq (MEK), which was founded as a Marxist movement and lobbies abroad as the National Council of Resistance of Iran. The MEK recently announced the formation of a provisional government in exile, hoping to capitalize on the current regime's instability. However, the group remains deeply unpopular inside Iran, largely due to its controversial decision to side with Iraq during the brutal 1980–1988 Iran-Iraq War. Additionally, heavily armed Kurdish militias and political groups concentrated in western Iran represent another formidable form of organized opposition. Unfortunately, their call for autonomous rule unnerves many Iranians who deeply fear the ethnic fragmentation of the state.

All these diverse opposition groups share a long, bitter history of fighting one another almost as fiercely as they fight the clerical regime. However, the recent outbreak of war has suddenly ignited an unprecedented effort to forge a united political front among the diaspora. The highly anticipated Iran Freedom Congress is scheduled to convene in London, marking a rare moment of collaboration. Preparations for this gathering began secretly between the tragic January massacre of civilian protesters and the start of the joint U.S.-Israeli air assault. Organizers managed to keep the complex planning entirely leak-free, a feat considered unique in modern Iranian exile politics.

After successfully demonstrating that such disparate groups can work together, democratic activists are now seeking strategic international support for a peaceful democratic transition. However, many prominent opposition figures remain highly skeptical of direct foreign military intervention in their domestic affairs. Meanwhile, in Washington, the spiraling political costs of the conflict are becoming increasingly apparent as the midterm elections rapidly approach. The joint strikes against Iran caused the single biggest disruption to global oil markets in history, sending gas prices soaring across the United States. Republicans are acutely aware that consistently pricey oil in an election year poses a severe threat to their slim congressional majorities.

Historical precedent clearly shows the peril of high commodity prices for the incumbent political party during midterm elections. In 2010, when oil reached similarly high levels, President Barack Obama saw his party lose 64 seats in the House. Nimble political messaging simply cannot mask the hard, everyday truths of high commodity prices at the local gas pump. The White House has offered confoundingly mixed messages about exactly when this war might end or what would definitively be considered a strategic victory. As voters begin paying closer attention to the elections, the economic fallout from the Iran conflict may ultimately dictate the balance of power in Congress.
